coredata.dc:title: Rare-earth metal-doped orange emissive photoluminescent carbon quantum dots for highly sensitive detection of Hg2+ ions: Multi-color imaging and real samples
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Mohandoss, Sonaimuthu
Priyadharshini, Alagarsamy
Velu, Kuppu Sakthi
Napoleon, Ayyakannu Arumugam
Roy, Prasanta
Ahmad, Naushad
Khan, Mohammad Rizwan
Palanisamy, Subramanian
You, SangGuan
Kim, Seong-Cheol
coredata.dc:description: A novel praseodymium (Pr3+)-doped carbon quantum dots (Pr-CQDs) were synthesized through a solvothermal process using citric acid, glutathione and praseodymium as a precursors. The Pr3+ ions were effectively coordinated with the carboxylate groups present on the surface of the CQDs and became integrated into the nano-graphene structure within the carbon core. The Pr-CQDs were sensibly analyzed through several spectroscopic and microscopic techniques, including UV–Vis, photoluminescence (PL), FT-IR spectroscopy, XRD, TEM, and XPS. These Pr-CQDs displayed excellent PL properties, emitting a distinct orange light at 575 nm when excited at 400 nm, achieving a quantum yield of 39.18 %. Morphological investigations showed that the Pr-CQDs were spherical in shape, well-dispersed, and had an average size of 4.94 ± 1.1 nm. Additionally, they exhibited stable PL across a broad pH range and in solutions with varying ionic strengths, demonstrating their robustness under different environmental conditions. When tested as optical sensors, The PL properties of Pr-CQDs displayed remarkable selectivity toward Hg2+ ions, which caused significant PL quenching, while other metal ions produced negligible responses. A linear relationship was established between the PL intensity and the concentration of Hg2+ ions (0–10 μM), with the sensor showing a detection limit as low as 90.3 nM, indicating high sensitivity. In addition, the biocompatibility of the Pr-CQDs were successfully used to multi-color image live cells as confirmed by a water-soluble tetrazolium salt (WST) assay, supporting their potential for use in biomedical applications. Moreover, the practical application, the Hg2+ ions was effectively measured using a spike and recovery method with real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pr3+
CQDs
Photoluminescence
Orange-emission
Hg2+ ions
Multi-color bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface and morphological properties of Pr-CQDs
Selectivity of metal ions by Pr-CQDs
Interference of Pr-CQDs/Hg2+ by metal ions

The authors would also like to thank the Researchers Supporting Project Number (RSPD2025R668), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption, (b) PL excitation/emission spectra for the Pr-CQDs and (c) PL emission spectra and (d) Normalized PL intensity profile of Pr-CQDs with variations of excitation wavelength from 350 nm to 480 nm (pH; 7.4 PBS, Pr-CQDs; 0.5 mg/mL, λexc ∼ 400 nm).
PL stability of Pr-CQDs with (a) pH, (b) NaCl ionic strength, (c) time irradiation, and (d) long time storage (pH; 7.4 PBS, Pr-CQDs; 0.5 mg/mL, λexc ∼ 400 nm).
(a) FTIR, (b) XRD, (c) TEM image (inset; HRTEM), and (b) the size distribution histograms of Pr-CQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of Pr-CQDs and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) Pr 3d, (c) N 1 s, (d) S 2p, (e) C 1 s, and (f) O 1 s elements.

(a) Selectivity measurement for the PL spectra of Pr-CQDs in the presence of various metal ions, (b) PL intensity of Pr-CQDs aqueous solutions in the presence and absence of different metal ions, and (c) Photographs of the aqueous Pr-CQDs containing various metal ions under daylight and UV light at 365 nm (pH; 7.4 PBS, Pr-CQDs; 0.5 mg/mL, λexc ∼ 400 nm).

(a) PL spectra of Pr-CQDs (0.5 mg/mL) dispersion in the presence of different Hg2+ concentrations (0–10 µM), (b) PL intensity profile of Pr-CQDs at 575 nm versus the concentrations of Hg2+, (c) The linear relation of P0/P with concentrations of Hg2+ in the range of 0–10 µM, and (d) The limit of detection based on the K=SD/S from the PL intensity of Pr-CQDs at 575 nm versus the concentrations of Hg2+ (0–10 µM). (pH; 7.4 PBS, Pr-CQDs; 0.5 mg/mL, λexc ∼ 400 nm).

(a and b) PL spectra and their enlarged PL intensity profile of the Pr-CQDs/Hg2+ system aqueous solution in the presence of different metal ions and (c) PL intensity histogram of the Pr-CQDs/Hg2+ system aqueous solution in the presence of different metal ions at 575 nm. (pH; 7.4 PBS, Pr-CQDs; 0.5 mg/mL, metal ions; 10 μM, λexc ∼ 400 nm).

(a) PL spectra and their corresponding PL color changes of Pr-CQDs, Pr-CQDs/Hg2+, and Pr-CQDs/Hg2++EDTA. (b) The PL reversibility of the Pr-CQDs by alternately adding Hg2+ ions and EDTA in pH ∼ 7.4. (pH; 7.4 PBS, Pr-CQDs; 0.5 mg/mL, Hg2+;10 μM, EDTA;20 μM, λexc ∼ 400 nm).

(a) Cell viability of HCT-116 cells treated with various concentrations of Pr-CQDs (0–80 μg/mL) for 24 h by WST assay and (b) Confocal laser scanning microscopy with bright-field, fluorescence (green and yellow channel), and merged images of HCT-116 cells incubated with Pr-CQDs and Pr-CQDs/Hg2+. Scale bar 50 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Preparation of Pr-doped carbon quantum dots (Pr-CQDs) and its Hg2+ sensing.

head.ce:title: Rare-earth metal-doped orange emissive photoluminescent carbon quantum dots for highly sensitive detection of Hg2+ ions: Multi-color imaging and real samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea (Sonaimuthu Mohandoss).

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors are contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel praseodymium (Pr3+)-doped carbon quantum dots (Pr-CQDs) were synthesized through a solvothermal process using citric acid, glutathione and praseodymium as a precursors. The Pr3+ ions were effectively coordinated with the carboxylate groups present on the surface of the CQDs and became integrated into the nano-graphene structure within the carbon core. The Pr-CQDs were sensibly analyzed through several spectroscopic and microscopic techniques, including UV–Vis, photoluminescence (PL), FT-IR spectroscopy, XRD, TEM, and XPS. These Pr-CQDs displayed excellent PL properties, emitting a distinct orange light at 575 nm when excited at 400 nm, achieving a quantum yield of 39.18 %. Morphological investigations showed that the Pr-CQDs were spherical in shape, well-dispersed, and had an average size of 4.94 ± 1.1 nm. Additionally, they exhibited stable PL across a broad pH range and in solutions with varying ionic strengths, demonstrating their robustness under different environmental conditions. When tested as optical sensors, The PL properties of Pr-CQDs displayed remarkable selectivity toward Hg2+ ions, which caused significant PL quenching, while other metal ions produced negligible responses. A linear relationship was established between the PL intensity and the concentration of Hg2+ ions (0–10 μM), with the sensor showing a detection limit as low as 90.3 nM, indicating high sensitivity. In addition, the biocompatibility of the Pr-CQDs were successfully used to multi-color image live cells as confirmed by a water-soluble tetrazolium salt (WST) assay, supporting their potential for use in biomedical applications. Moreover, the practical application, the Hg2+ ions was effectively measured using a spike and recovery method with real water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution, particularly from mercury (Hg2+), has garnered increasing attention due to its serious environmental and health risks [1]. Mercury, found in both inorganic salts and organic compounds like methylmercury, is released into the environment primarily through industrial activities and coal-burning power plants, contaminating soil, water, and air [2]. This pollutant poses severe risks to human health, including neurological disorders, kidney damage, and digestive system harm, especially in vulnerable populations like children and pregnant women [3]. As Hg2+ bioaccumulates through the food chain, even small amounts can lead to long-term health issues, making it a global threat to both ecosystems and human well-being. Due to the significant concerns over mercury contamination, there is increasing emphasis on detecting areas affected by Hg2+. Several analytical techniques are currently employed for Hg2+ detection, including selective atomic absorption spectrometry/atomic emission spectrometry [4], capillary electrophoresis [5], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [6]. While these methods offer high selectivity and sensitivity, they still face challenges in terms of sample preparation, cost-effectiveness, and the need for complex instrumentation. Among the various Hg2+ detection methods, fluorimetry is becoming increasingly popular due to its advantages, including high sensitivity, ease of use, and quick response times [7–9]. Many fluorescence-based systems have been developed for detecting Hg2+, such as DNA enzymes, polymers, single-walled carbon nanotubes, metal nanoclusters, and semiconductor quantum dots [10]. However, most of these sensors depend on changes in the intensity of the luminescence signal from a single emission center, which can lead to inaccurate results if there are variations in the external environment.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a unique class of fluorescent nanomaterials, have gained significant consideration due to their outstanding photoluminescence characteristics, high biocompatibility, and low toxicity [11]. CQDs are typically synthesized using two main approaches: the top-down and bottom-up methods. Among these, the bottom-up approach is more commonly employed due to its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and the precise control it offers over the final properties of the CQDs [12]. This method involves converting small organic molecules into CQDs through chemical reactions, often utilizing biomass as a precursor. Biomass is especially suitable because it primarily consists of small organic molecules that can undergo carbonization at elevated temperatures. Bottom-up synthesis techniques include thermal decomposition, hydrothermal and solvothermal carbonization, and microwave irradiation [13]. These methods provide excellent control over the shape, size, and other characteristics of the resulting nanomaterials. By tailoring the reaction conditions, the bottom-up approach ensures the production of CQDs with highly desirable properties for a variety of applications. The solvothermal method offers significant advantages over the hydrothermal approach for the synthesis of CQDs, particularly when water is not used as the reaction medium [14]. Unlike the hydrothermal method, which relies on water as the solvent, the solvothermal method utilizes organic solvents such as dimethylformamide, ethanol, or acetone. These solvents provide greater flexibility in tuning the reaction environment, enabling better control over the size, shape, and surface properties of the resulting CQDs [15]. Organic solvents often participate directly in the reaction, introducing functional groups that enhance the photoluminescence and solubility of the CQDs in non-aqueous systems. Additionally, the solvothermal method allows for improved reaction control through adjustable parameters such as solvent polarity and boiling point, which influence the CQDs optical and structural characteristics [16]. This method also yields CQDs with higher quantum yields and tunable emissions, making them more suitable for advanced applications in optoelectronics, organic-phase sensors, and light-emitting devices.

Rare earth metals-doped with CQDs, such as Praseodymium (Pr), Europium (Eu), Terbium (Tb), or Gadolinium (Gd), are enhanced with unique luminescent properties [17181920]. These potentials make rare earth-doped CQDs ideal for applications requiring stable and reliable optical properties, such as bioimaging, light-emitting devices, and security printing [21]. Doping with rare earth metals introduces defect states, boosts quantum efficiency, and broadens the emission spectrum, which enhances their utility in fields like photoluminescence, catalysis, environmental monitoring, and biomedical imaging. Such CQDs serve as a versatile material that bridges the gap between nanotechnology and practical, multifunctional applications. Recent research highlights the integration of carbon dots with rare earth ions. For example, Sreedhara et al. suggested that combining Pr3+-doped gahnite nanophosphors with carbon dots could improve photoluminescent properties, facilitating applications in thermal sensing, data protection, and forensic fingerprint imaging [17]. The interaction between carbon dots and Pr3+ ions may enable luminescence that responds to temperature changes, enhance data encryption, and provide high-contrast imaging for forensic purposes. Similarly, Correia et al. demonstrated that Eu(III)-doped carbon dots could function as dual fluorescent nanosensors for selectively detecting Ag+ and Hg2+ ions with high sensitivity [18]. The unique luminescent traits of Eu(III) ions and their interaction with target ions produce distinct fluorescence responses, making these sensors efficient for real-time environmental and analytical monitoring. In another study, Mondal et al. developed a Tb-doped carbon quantum dot nanocomposite with dual-emissive properties for detecting Hg2+ ions in water [19]. This composite exhibits fluorescence changes upon Hg2+ binding, offering a reliable visual indicator for environmental assessments. Furthermore, Hu et al. reported that gadolinium-doped carbon dots paired with carbon microparticles could act as a “turn-off–on” fluorescent probe for glucose detection, using fluorescence quenching and recovery mechanisms to enable accurate, real-time glucose monitoring [20]. Among these developments, Pr3+-doped CQDs have gained attention as effective luminescent probes. They emit red and green–blue light, significantly broadening their operational range while enhancing sensitivity and precision [17]. Pr3+ ions are particularly appealing due to their cost-effectiveness, facilitating large-scale production of Pr3+-doped CQDs for widespread use. The emission properties of Pr3+-doped CQDs, spanning ultraviolet to infrared, result from the ion’s closely spaced energy levels and variable energy gaps, enabling applications across diverse fields.

The aim of these work is to develop a photoluminescent sensor for Hg2+ ions using Pr3+-doped carbon quantum dots (Pr-CQDs) and were further explored as cell imaging for biomedical applications. Herein, we synthesized Pr-CQDs using a solvothermal method with citric acid, glutathione, and praseodymium nitrate as the primary precursors (Scheme 1 ). The synthesized Pr-CQDs were thoroughly characterized using several techniques, including UV–vis, PL, FT-IR, XPS, and TEM analysis. The Pr-CQDs displayed a strong orange emission with a prominent peak at 575 nm and an impressive quantum yield (QY) of 39.18 %. These Pr-CQDs demonstrated excellent recognition capabilities for Hg2+ ions through a “PL-off” mechanism, showing high selectivity for Hg2+ without interference from other commonly encountered metal ions. The presence of Hg2+ significantly quenched the PL of the CQDs, while leaving the PL emission of the Pr-CQDs unaffected. This unique property enabled the Pr-CQDs to serve as the recognition and reference components, respectively, for constructing a PL sensor to detect Hg2+ in aqueous solutions. The resulting PL Pr-CQDs probe exhibited a broad linear detection range of 0–10 µM (R2 = 0.9859) and an exceptionally low detection limit of 90.3 nM. Moreover, the high selectivity and sensitivity of the probe, Pr-CQDs towards Hg2+ ions have been utilized for in vitro cell imaging and real sample analysis. To the best of our knowledge, our designed chemosensor Pr-CQDs, for the first time, is successfully utilized for sensitive detection of Hg2+ ions with excellent sensing properties, multi-color cell imaging and environmental monitoring.

Citric acid and glutathione were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Praseodymium and all the aqueous solutions of metal ions were prepared from nitrate and chloride salts were purchased from TCI chemicals Co. Ltd, South Korea. All chemicals and solvents used were of analytical grade and were employed without any additional purification.

The Pr-CQDs were synthesized using citric acid, glutathione, and praseodymium nitrate as precursors through a straightforward one-step solvothermal method. In this process, 0.10 g citric acid, 0.15 g glutathione, and 0.15 g praseodymium nitrate were dissolved in a 50 mL solution of dimethyl formamide and stirred until a clear solution was obtained. The resulting solution was transferred into 80 mL stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 120 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was carefully collected, filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane, and subjected to dialysis with a cellulose ester membrane (molecular weight cutoff: 500–1000 Da) for 24 h. The final product was stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

The quantum yields (ɸ) of the Pr3+-doped CQDs were determined using quinine sulfate (ɸst = 0.54) as the reference standard. The integrated PL intensity for Pr3+-doped CQDs was obtained by measuring the area under the emission curve with a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer. The quantum yields were then calculated using the following formula:
ϕ
sam
=
ϕ
ref
×
A
ref
A
sam
×
I
sam
I
ref
×
ƞ
sam
ƞ
ref
2

In the formula, the subscripts “sam” and “ref” represent the sample and the reference standard, respectively. ɸsam and ɸref the quantum yields of the sample and the reference standard (QY 0.54 in 0.1 N H2SO4) respectively. A refers to the absorbance, I is the integrated emission intensity at the same excitation wavelength (400 nm), and ƞ represents the refractive index, where ƞsam = 1.33 and ƞref = 1.36. The UV absorbance was maintained below 0.1 to ensure accurate measurements.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of Pr-CQDs was used to analyze the sensing of various cations. A series of solutions containing different metal ions (Ag+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Ce3+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Na+, Sn2+, Sr2+, and Ca2+) were systematically combined with Pr-CQDs. pH ∼ 7.4 PBS solution was utilized to detect Hg2+ ions at ambient conditions. For Hg2+ detection, 20 μL of Pr-CQDs (0.5 mg/mL) were mixed with 1.97 mL of PBS buffer (pH 7.4) and subsequently, 10 μL of a stock solution containing known concentrations of Hg2+ ions (ranging from 0 to 10 μM) was added to the cuvette. The mixture was then gently shaken for 5 min to ensure uniformity. For the selectivity test, the prepared Pr-CQDs solution (0.5 mg/mL) was combined with solutions containing specific concentrations of each metal ion. This allowed for a detailed investigation of the sensing capabilities of Pr-CQDs. PL spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 400–750 nm with an excitation wavelength of 400 nm.

The optical properties of the synthesized Pr-CQDs were analyzed using UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectrum displays distinct and intense absorption peaks at 272 nm, likely corresponding to the π − π* transitions associated with −C=N and −C=O bonds [22]. Additionally, a weak and broad absorption band observed peak at 370 nm may be attributed to the n − π* transitions of Pr-CQDs (Fig. 1 a) [23]. This observation aligns with the orange emissive PL exhibited by the Pr-CQDs solution under UV irradiation at 365 nm. The inset of Fig. 1a further illustrates the Pr-CQDs solutions, appearing yellow color under daylight and emitting a bright orange color when exposed to (365 nm) UV light. The PL excitation/emission spectrum of Pr-CQDs were verified under an excitation wavelength of 400 nm, is presented in Fig. 1b. It reveals a prominent emission peak at 575 nm, confirming the orange emissive PL Pr-CQDs. Moreover, the PL spectroscopy was performed on the synthesized Pr-CQDs across a range of excitation wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 1c.

Additionally, as the excitation wavelength increases from 350 to 480 nm, the emission peak of Pr-CQDs gradually shifts toward longer wavelengths, demonstrating a clear wavelength-dependent emission behavior [24]. The PL spectra (Fig. 1c) of the aqueous Pr-CQDs have been converted into a 2D excitation–emission contour map, as illustrated in Fig. S1. When excited at 365 nm under UV light irradiation, the GQD exhibits a bright green fluorescence (inset Fig. S1) is obtained. A shift in the normalized PL peak from 350 nm to 480 nm demonstrates a redshift, indicating that the emission wavelength progressively moves toward longer wavelengths, corresponding to lower energy levels (Fig. 1d). This shift indicates a wavelength-dependent emission behavior, with the peak positions moving toward longer wavelengths, reflecting a redshift in the emission spectrum. This behavior implies that the PL properties of Pr-CQDs are affected by the particle size and color, likely due to surface states. The excitation-dependent PL characteristics suggest the existence of different surface defects and states within the Pr-CQDs.

To ensure optimal stability, the experimental parameters, including pH, ionic strength, irradiation duration, and long-term storage conditions, were precisely adjusted and optimized. The optical response of the prepared Pr-CQDs was evaluated across a range of pH values to study the effect of pH on their PL behavior. PL intensity measurements were performed using a spectrofluorometer on Pr-CQDs solutions with pH values spanning from 3 to 12. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the highest emission intensity was observed at pH 7. However, a significant increase in intensity occurred as the pH increased from 3 to 7, followed by a relatively significant decline between pH 8 to 12 [25]. At lower pH values (acidic conditions), excess protons interact with surface oxygen-containing groups such as hydroxyl (–OH) and carboxyl (–COOH), leading to a change in surface charge distribution and altering the quantum confinement effect. This results in a shift in energy levels, affecting electron transition dynamics and subsequently influencing the photoluminescence (PL) intensity and emission wavelength. As the pH increases towards neutral conditions, the deprotonation of these functional groups enhances surface passivation, reducing non-radiative recombination and leading to stronger PL emission, as observed at pH 7. However, at higher pH values (alkaline conditions), further deprotonation can induce structural rearrangements and potential aggregation of the Pr-CQDs, altering their electronic states and increasing non-radiative decay pathways, thereby causing a decline in PL intensity. Additionally, changes in the local dielectric environment at different pH levels can influence the electronic bandgap, resulting in slight wavelength shifts. These combined effects illustrate the intricate relationship between pH and the optical properties of Pr-CQDs, demonstrating their sensitivity to environmental conditions. Furthermore, the PL emission of Pr-CQDs in the high salt concentrations was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2b, the PL intensity remained largely unchanged under different ionic conditions. Even at NaCl from 0 to 1.0 M ranging concentrations, no significant decrease in PL intensity was observed [26]. The PL intensity of Pr-CQDs demonstrated remarkable stability under irradiation. After 20 min of continuous radiation exposure, the PL intensity exhibited only a minor decline, retaining about 93 % of its original value as shown in Fig. 2c [27]. This high level of photostability highlights the robustness of Pr-CQDs and their potential for applications requiring extended illumination, such as bioimaging or optoelectronic devices. In addition, the PL intensity of the Pr-CQDs was measured on day 0 from establish a baseline. After 90 days of storage, the PL intensity showed only a slight reduction, retaining a significant percentage of its original value (Fig. 2d). This indicates excellent long-term stability, making Pr-CQDs suitable for applications requiring prolonged storage without significant loss of functionality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface and morphological properties of Pr-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The structural characteristics of Pr-CQDs were comprehensively studied using a range of advanced characterization methods such as FTIR, XRD, and TEM. Fig. 3 a shows the FT-IR spectra of the synthesized Pr-CQDs. A broad absorption band between 3500 and 3000 cm−1 is ascribed to the O–H bond stretching vibrations in carboxyl groups and the N–H bond in amino groups [28]. A peak observed at approximately 2900 cm−1 in the spectra agrees the presence of aliphatic hydrocarbon chains on the surface of the Pr-CQDs, corresponding to the C–H stretching vibrations. Additionally, a distinct peak around 1650 cm−1 signifies the stretching vibrations of carbonyl groups (C=O), further confirming their presence on the surface of the Pr-CQDs [29]. The peaks at 2471 cm−1 and 1180 cm−1 confirm the presence of S–H and C–S bonds, respectively. Peaks observed at 1600 cm−1 and 1399 cm−1 are associated with the vibrational modes of C=C bonds. Additionally, the absorption band in the range of 1240–1390 cm−1 corresponds to the C–N bond stretching vibrations. The band observed at 1031 cm−1 agrees to the C–O–C bending vibrations. Additionally, a band at 860 cm−1 is corresponding to the C–O bond stretching vibrations, while a band at 471 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of the Pr–O bond [30], suggesting the presence of praseodymium-oxygen interactions within the material.

The structure of the Pr-CQDs was further analyzed using XRD measurement. The XRD analysis of the synthesized Pr-CQDs, shown in Fig. 3b, reveals a broad hump at a 2θ value of 22.4° closely resembling the diffraction peak associated with graphite structures typically reported in the range of 23–24°, corresponding to the (002) plane of amorphous carbon [31]. In addition, the Pr-CQDs showed shoulder peak 2θ patterns at 43.4°, which was assigned to disordered carbon atoms with a high degree of (100) plane for the hexagonal graphite structure [31]. The morphology and size of the synthesized Pr-CQDs were analyzed using TEM. The TEM image (Fig. 3c) reveal that the synthesized Pr-CQDs are quasi-spherical, uniform, and monodisperse. HRTEM image further confirm that all Pr-CQDs exhibit well-defined lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [31]. The Pr-CQDs exhibit a spherical shape and are well-dispersed, with sizes ranging from 3 to 8 nm. The average diameters of the Pr-CQDs are measured to be 4.94 ± 1.1 nm as shown in Fig. 3d.

XPS was utilized to investigate the elemental composition of Pr-CQDs. The survey spectrum displays five distinct peaks corresponding to Pr 3d, N 1 s, S 2p, C 1 s, and O 1 s, located at binding energies of approximately 933 eV, 401 eV, 168 eV, 285 eV, and 532 eV, respectively (Fig. 4 a). The Pr 3d spectra of Pr-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 4b, reveal two prominent peaks found at binding energies of 935.6 eV and 956.3 eV. These peaks are ascribed to Pr 3d5/2 and Pr 3d3/2, respectively, indicating the characteristic electronic states of praseodymium [31]. In Fig. 4c, the N1s spectrum deconvoluted the existence of N–H (399.4 eV), N–C (400.3 eV), and C–N–C (401.8 eV) [32].

The deconvoluted S2p spectrum in Fig. 4d displays three distinct peaks. The peaks located at 159.8 eV and 162.6 eV are attributed to the spin–orbit coupling of 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 states in the C–S bond. Meanwhile, the peak observed at 167.9 eV is associated with the oxidized sulfur present in the S=O bond [33]. The C 1 s (Fig. 4e) reveal four distinct peaks located at 284.3, 285.1, 286.4, and 287.8 eV. These peaks agree to various chemical bonds: C=C/C−C representing graphitic carbon, C−N/C−S, C−OH, and C=O, respectively. The O 1 s region of Pr-CQDs, shown in Fig. 4f, exhibits three distinct peaks at 531.4 and 532.9 eV corresponding to the C=O and C−O bonds, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of metal ions by Pr-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the sensing abilities of Pr-CQDs, selectivity tests were performed using pH 7.4 PBS buffer. The PL intensities of Pr-CQDs were analyzed by introducing various metal ions, such as Ag+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Ce3+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Na+, Sn2+, Sr2+, and Ca2+, at a concentration of 10 µM to the Pr-CQDs solutions (0.5 mg/mL). As depicted in Fig. 5 a and 5b, Hg2+ demonstrated a pronounced quenching effect on the PL of Pr-CQDs, while no notable PL changes were observed with other metal ions [34]. This result highlights the role of Pr-CQDs as Hg2+-specific chemosensors, effectively turn-off PL Pr-CQDs in the presence of Hg2+. The selective interaction between Pr-CQDs and Hg2+ was clearly evident in solutions containing various cations. The chemosensor solution, consisting of Pr-CQDs (0.5 mg/mL), was mixed with Hg2+ ions (10 µM) in an aqueous medium and exposed to UV light at 365 nm. This resulted in a distinct “turn-off” PL emission, shifting from orange to colorless, as illustrated in Fig. 5c.

To evaluate the sensitivity of the system, varying concentrations of Hg2+ (0 to 10 μM) were added to the Pr-CQDs solution, and the PL emission intensity was monitored. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the PL intensity of Pr-CQDs at 575 nm progressively decreased with increasing Hg2+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 10 μM, indicating that Hg2+ effectively quenched the PL of Pr-CQDs [19,35]. The reduction in PL intensity was quantitatively correlated with the Hg2+ concentrations and was accompanied by noticeable color shifts in PL emission (inset of Fig. 6a). These results demonstrate that the Pr-CQDs sensor can quantitatively and sensitively detect trace levels of Hg2+ in water. Job's plot analysis, used to define the binding stoichiometry, revealed that Pr-CQDs interact with Hg2+ ions at maximum PL intensity with a mole fraction of 0.5. This result confirms a 1:1 stoichiometric ratio for the formation of Pr-CQDs–Hg2+ complexes (Fig. 6b) [36,37]. As shown in Fig. 6c (inset), the data followed a linear relationship described by the equation y = 0.1953x, with a strong correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9859). The linear range for the quantitative detection of Hg2+ ions was determined to be 0–10 μM, with a detection limit (LOD) of 90.3 nM (Fig. 6d). When utilized in its entirety, the chemosensor Pr-CQDs demonstrates high sensitivity in detecting Hg2+ ions through PL signals [18,38]. The estimated detection limit is 90.3 nM, which is considerably lower than the values reported in previous studies (as shown in Table S1).

The detection mechanism of Pr-CQDs for Hg2+ ions is based on a combination of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) leading to a PL “turn-off” response. The Pr-CQDs possess various oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups, such as carboxyl (–COOH), hydroxyl (–OH), and amines (–NH2), which serve as strong binding sites for Hg2+ ions. The selective interaction between Hg2+ ions and these functional groups induces a PET, where excited-state electrons in Pr-CQDs are transferred to Hg2+ ions, causing non-radiative energy dissipation and quenching the PL signal. This dual quenching mechanism results in a highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+ ions, with Pr-CQDs displaying a linear PL quenching response in the 0–10 μM range and a detection limit of 90.3 nM. The PL color change from bright orange to colorless under UV light further enhances its practicality for real-time Hg2+ ions detection in environmental and biological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference of Pr-CQDs/Hg2+ by metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The ability to selectively identify a target analyte in the presence of similar analytes is a critical characteristic of nanosensors for analytical applications. Consequently, it was essential to evaluate the high selectivity of the Pr-CQDs probe for specific metal ions. In this context, the detection of Hg2+ ions in environmental samples are particularly important. The study focused on assessing the Pr-CQDs selectivity for Hg2+ ions, even in the presence of other reactive components in the samples. To evaluate the selectivity of Pr-CQDs for Hg2+ ions, interference studies were conducted with other competing metal ions (Fig. 7 a,b).

In this study, an aqueous solution of Pr-CQDs (0.5 mg/mL) was treated with Hg2+ ions (10 µM), along with other metal ions (10 µM) added separately (Ag+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Ce3+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Na+, Sn2+, Sr2+, and Ca2+). The PL spectra were recorded, and the changes in PL intensity of Pr-CQDs in the presence and absence of the metal ions, respectively. The results showed minimal or no significant change in PL intensity for Pr-CQDs with Hg2+ ions in the presence of other metal ions [36]. Notably, Pr-CQDs exhibited a specific response only to Hg2+ ions. These findings confirm that other competing metal ions do not interfere with the selectivity of Pr-CQDs for detecting Hg2+ ions. The PL histogram data (Fig. 7c) further highlights the sensor capability to accurately differentiate Hg2+ ions from other cations. The significant PL quenching observed in the presence of Hg2+ ions, even among competing species, emphasizes the sensors exceptional sensitivity and specificity.

Reversibility and regeneration are essential features of PL chemosensors for practical applications, enabling their reuse across multiple cycles. The interaction between Pr-CQDs and Hg2+ ions was found to be reversible, as confirmed by experiments involving EDTA, a well-known metal ion chelating agent [39]. The reversibility of Pr-CQDs binding during Hg2+ detection was demonstrated in the presence of EDTA. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the PL intensity of Pr-CQDs, initially interacting with an equivalent concentration of Hg2+ ions (10 μM), increased upon the incremental addition of EDTA (20 μM). This observation clearly indicates that the Pr-CQDs sensor interaction with Hg2+ ions is reversible in nature. Additionally, the PL color of the Pr-CQDs/Hg2+ complex shifted from colorless to orange upon EDTA addition (inset of Fig. 8a), further confirming the restoration of the Pr-CQDs to their original state [40]. To evaluate the reproducibility of this reversibility, the Pr-CQDs underwent ten cycles of alternating additions of Hg2+ ions and EDTA (Fig. 8b). After each cycle, the PL signal returned to orange, with over 90 % of the original PL intensity consistently recovered across all cycles. This demonstrates the strong regenerative capability of the Pr-CQDs sensor.

To evaluate the practical application of the chemosensor in biological contexts, cytotoxicity tests were performed using HCT-116 cells treated with different concentrations of Pr-CQDs, ranging from 0 to 80 μg/mL. The WST assay results revealed that the viability of HCT-116 cells remained above 98 % at all tested concentrations [41], indicating that the Pr-CQDs were non-cytotoxic under the experimental conditions (Fig. 9 a). Furthermore, HCT-116 cells were incubated with Pr-CQDs at 80 μg/mL for 15 min at 37 °C. After incubation, the cells were washed three times with PBS and analyzed using CLSM to measure their suitability for cellular imaging. As shown in Fig. 9b, no detectable emission signal was observed from HCT-116 cells in the bright field when Pr-CQDs were absent. However, upon incubating the cells with a Pr-CQDs aqueous solution for 30 min at 37 °C, the free sensor successfully labelled with Pr-CQDs (80 μg/mL) demonstrated distinct intracellular fluorescence [42]. Specifically, under excitation at 488 nm (green channel) and 550 nm (yellowish-orange channel) wavelength, the cells exhibited dark green and bright yellowish-orange fluorescence, respectively, highlighting the effective labeling and imaging capabilities of the Pr-CQDs within the cellular environment [43,44].

Upon further incubation with Hg2+ ions, live cells exhibited a significant reduction in green and yellowish-orange fluorescence, indicating quenched emission signals [36]. Detailed analysis using fluorescence and bright-field overlay images revealed that the fluorescence signals were primarily localized within intracellular compartments. This observation suggests that both the Pr-CQDs and Pr-CQDs/Hg2+ complex demonstrated efficient cell membrane permeability. These results highlight the potential of the synthesized Pr-CQDs as effective fluorescence sensors for detecting Hg2+ in biological systems. This enhances imaging quality and improves the efficiency of intracellular labeling. Based on these findings underscore the excellent biocompatibility of Pr-CQDs, establishing them as promising candidates for safe and effective bioimaging applications. Their potential utility in biological and medical research further solidifies their significance in advancing fluorescence-based diagnostic and therapeutic techniques.

The practical utility of the proposed sensor probe Pr-CQDs was evaluated by measuring Hg2+ ions concentrations in tap and lake water samples. Lake water samples were collected from the premises of Gyeongsan High School, while tap water samples were sourced from our laboratory at Yeungnam University, South Korea. For the analysis, standard solutions of Hg2+ ions were spiked into the water samples to create three distinct concentration levels. PL emission intensities were measured, showing a strong correlation with the ion concentrations within the range of 1, 5, and 10 μM (Table S2). Each experiment was repeated three times for accuracy, yielding a maximum recovery rate of 104.0 % and a relative standard deviation ranging from 0.98 % to 2.31 %. These results demonstrated the reliability and practicality of the label-free Pr-CQDs probe for detecting Hg2+ ions. Furthermore, this probe also proved effective in tracking Hg2+ ions in other real samples, showcasing its potential as a versatile and dependable tool for environmental and analytical applications.

Beyond Hg2+ ions detection, bioimaging and real water sample, the study suggests that Pr-CQDs hold immense potential for diverse applications in environmental monitoring, optoelectronics, catalysis, and biomedicine. Their high photoluminescence quantum yield (39.18 %), excellent water solubility, stability under varying conditions, and minimal toxicity make them promising candidates for detecting other heavy metal ions, organic pollutants, and biomolecules in water and biological systems. Additionally, due to their unique optical properties, Pr-CQDs could be utilized in the development of PL probes for disease diagnostics, targeted drug delivery, and photothermal therapy. Their efficient electron transfer capabilities also suggest applications in solar cells, LEDs, and photocatalysis, where they could enhance light absorption and charge separation processes. Furthermore, the ability of Pr-CQDs to undergo reversible interactions with metal ions, as demonstrated with EDTA, could pave the way for their use in smart sensing devices and real-time monitoring systems for environmental and healthcare applications. Finally, the future research may focus on modifying Pr-CQDs with functional materials to expand their usability in nanomedicine, biosensing, and energy storage devices, unlocking new avenues for advanced technological innovations.

In conclusion, the Praseodymium-doped photoluminescent carbon quantum dots (Pr-CQDs), synthesized through a one-step solvothermal method, exhibited remarkable properties for detecting Hg2+ ions and demonstrated potential for biomedical applications. The synthesized Pr-CQDs were thoroughly characterized using UV–vis, PL, FTIR, XRD, TEM, and XPS. These techniques confirmed the optical properties, morphology, and surface composition of the material, highlighting the presence of various functional groups containing praseodymium. High-resolution TEM images revealed that the Pr-CQDs had an average size of approximately 4.94 ± 1.1 nm, with a size range of 3–8 nm and an interlayer distance of 0.21 nm. The synthesized Pr-CQDs demonstrated remarkable stability across a range of conditions, including varying pH levels, ionic strength of salts, and exposure to UV radiation. Additionally, they exhibited high quantum yields reaching up to 39.18 %. In the presence of Hg2+ ions, the Pr-CQDs exhibited significant PL quenching and showed high selectivity over the metal ions. The sensor demonstrated a linear PL response to Hg2+ ion concentrations within the range of 0–10 μM, with a detection limit of 90.3 nM. The Pr-CQDs chemosensors were found to coordinate with Hg2+ ions in a 1:1 stoichiometric ratio, offering highly sensitive and selective PL responses to Hg2+ ions even in the presence of other interfering ions in water. Notably, the PL Pr-CQDs probe was successfully utilized for the detection of Hg2+ ions in live cells via multicolor imaging using HCT-116 cells. Additionally, the Pr-CQDs chemosensor demonstrated its practical utility by enabling the simultaneous detection of trace amounts of Hg2+ ions in real water samples. The Pr-CQDs low cost, excellent biocompatibility, minimal toxicity, and distinctive optical properties position them as versatile materials with significant potential for diverse applications, warranting further exploration through future research.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank for the research work that the Basic Science Research Program supported through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), funded by the Ministry of Education (2020R1I1A3052258). The authors would also like to thank the Researchers Supporting Project Number (RSPD2025R668), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
